Nestled amidst Cambodia’s ancient Angkor temples, the enigmatic Neak Pean has a fascinating history. Built in the late 12th century during the reign of King Jayavarman VII, this unique temple served as a medicinal sanctuary where pilgrims bathed in its healing waters. Originally named Rajyasri (“king’s glory”), it was later rededicated to the Buddhist bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara as the empire shifted to Mahayana Buddhism.
